content:
# Acts Chapter 3 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 227-228 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_003_page_001.jpg, ACT_003_page_002.jpg

---

### The Healing at the Temple Gate (vv. 1-10)

**[1]** One afternoon, Peter and John were heading up to the temple for the three o'clock hour of prayer. **[2]** At the gate called Beautiful, a man who had been unable to walk from the day he was born was being carried there — as he was every day — so he could beg from people entering the temple. **[3]** When he saw Peter and John about to go in, he asked them for money.

**[4]** Peter looked straight at him, and so did John. "Look at us," Peter said. **[5]** The man gave them his full attention, expecting to receive something.

**[6]** But Peter said, "I don't have silver or gold — but what I do have, I give you: In the name of Jesus the Messiah of Nazareth, get up and walk!" **[7]** He grabbed the man's right hand and pulled him up. Instantly, strength surged into his feet and ankles. **[8]** He jumped to his feet and began to walk. Then he went into the temple with them — walking, leaping, and praising God.

**[9]** All the people saw him walking and praising God, **[10]** and when they recognized him as the man who used to sit begging at the Beautiful Gate, they were completely overwhelmed — stunned by what had happened to him.

### Peter's Address at Solomon's Colonnade (vv. 11-26)

**[11]** While the man clung to Peter and John, all the people came running to them at what is called Solomon's Colonnade, utterly astonished.

**[12]** When Peter saw this, he spoke to the crowd: "People of Israel, why are you so amazed by this? Why do you stare at us as though we made this man walk by our own power or godliness? **[13]** The God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob — the God of our ancestors — has glorified his servant Jesus. You handed him over and rejected him before Pilate, even though Pilate had decided to release him. **[14]** You rejected the Holy and Righteous One and demanded that a murderer be granted to you instead. **[15]** You killed the Author of life — but God raised him from the dead, and we are witnesses of this.

**[16]** "It is the name of Jesus — and the faith that comes through his name — that has given this man the strength you see and know. The faith that comes through Jesus has given him this complete healing, right here in front of all of you.

**[17]** "Now, brothers, I know that you acted in ignorance, as did your leaders. **[18]** But this is how God fulfilled what he had announced through all the prophets: that his Messiah would suffer. **[19]** So turn your hearts back to God and turn away from your sins, so that your sins may be wiped clean — **[20]** and so that times of refreshing may come from the presence of the Lord, and he may send the Messiah appointed for you — Jesus. **[21]** Heaven must receive him until the time comes for the restoration of all things, which God spoke about long ago through his holy prophets.

**[22]** "Moses said, *'The Lord your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among your own people. You must listen to everything he tells you.* **[23]** *Anyone who does not listen to that prophet will be completely cut off from the people.'*

**[24]** "Indeed, all the prophets from Samuel onward — every one who spoke — foretold these days. **[25]** You are the heirs of the prophets and of the covenant God made with your ancestors when he said to Abraham, *'Through your offspring all the families of the earth will be blessed.'* **[26]** When God raised up his servant, he sent him to you first — to bless you by turning each of you from your wicked ways."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **v. 2 — "Beautiful Gate" (Ωραια):** The Sinaiticus reads ωραια (Beautiful/Lovely). This gate, likely the Nicanor Gate on the temple's eastern side, was famous for its Corinthian bronze. I retained the traditional name; the irony of a broken man at the "Beautiful" gate is already embedded in the narrative.

2. **v. 6 — "I don't have silver or gold":** The Greek αργυριον η χρυσιον (silver or gold) is rendered as a direct, colloquial statement to capture Peter's bluntness. He is not apologizing — he is setting up the real gift.

3. **v. 7 — "strength surged into":** The Greek εστερεωθησαν (were made firm/strong) describes an instantaneous physical transformation. "Surged" conveys the sudden, dramatic nature of the healing better than the clinical "were strengthened."

4. **v. 14 — "Author of life" (τον αρχηγον της ζωης):** αρχηγος means originator, founder, pioneer, or prince. "Author of life" captures the paradox Peter is driving at: you killed the very one who is the source of all life. This is the theological crux of the sermon.

5. **v. 15 — Sinaiticus reading:** The Sinaiticus text at this point reads consistently with the standard text tradition for Acts 3, without major variants affecting translation.

6. **v. 19 — "turn your hearts back" for μετανοησατε:** Rather than the churchified "repent," I used "turn your hearts back" to capture the Hebrew concept behind the Greek — a full reorientation of one's life direction toward God. Combined with επιστρεψατε (turn back), the double imperative demands a total about-face.

7. **v. 20 — "times of refreshing" (καιροι αναψυξεως):** αναψυξις literally means "a breathing again" or "cooling relief." This is a striking metaphor — as if the people have been holding their breath or living in suffocating heat. The promised relief comes from God's own presence. I kept the traditional rendering as it carries sufficient poetic weight.

8. **v. 21 — "restoration of all things" (αποκαταστασεως παντων):** This phrase carries enormous theological weight — the final renewal of all creation. It echoes prophetic promises of cosmic restoration and is one of the most sweeping eschatological statements in Acts.

9. **v. 25 — "heirs" for υιοι (sons):** The Greek literally says "sons of the prophets and of the covenant." In this context, the meaning is clearly one of inheritance and belonging, not biological descent. "Heirs" communicates the intended force to a modern audience.

10. **v. 26 — "raised up" (αναστησας):** This verb is deliberately ambiguous in Greek — it can mean both "raised up" (as in calling into service) and "raised from the dead." Peter likely intends both senses: God raised Jesus from death and sent him as the promised servant. I retained the ambiguity with "raised up."

— PNEUMA
